title: Prepare approval documents - Procurement plan | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Procurement risk assessment for complexity level 1, 2 and

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: Procurement plan

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: A procurement plan outlines the intended strategy for conducting procurement activities prior to undertaking significant actions such as posting an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) or entering into contract negotiations.

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: The procurement plan is a key tool to be used in discussions and to review points for considering options like competitive versus sole source, and industrial and regional benefits.

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: Before issuing a solicitation of offers, you must obtain approval of the procurement plan at the appropriate level.

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: A Procurement Risk Assessment (PRA) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - PSPC only) must be performed prior to completing the procurement plan and be attached to it. The identification of risks supports the formulation of risk mitigation strategies.

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: There are two types of procurement plans:

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: Procurement plan for procurements at or below Director General (DG) approval level

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement plan
structure_blocks.text: Formal Procurement Plan Template for procurements at or above Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) level

It must include the elements identified under the formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM level.

structure_blocks.heading: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level
heading_path: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level
structure_blocks.text: The procurement plan must provide details on medium-high to high risks and proposed mitigation strategies. You may also refer to Determine the procurement strategy for a list of items that could be included in the procurement plan.

structure_blocks.heading: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level

structure_blocks.heading: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level

structure_blocks.text: Submission to the designated authority at or below the DG level for review of the procurement plan and the PRA , addressing comprehensive risk identification and mitigation

structure_blocks.heading: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level
heading_path: For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level
structure_blocks.text: Formal approval by the DG or their authorized representative, ensuring that all procedural and strategic aspects of the procurement are adequately addressed and documented

structure_blocks.heading: Formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM approval level
heading_path: Formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM approval level
structure_blocks.text: The formal procurement plan is required for the approval of the procurement strategy for all Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Acquisitions Program (AP) procurements requiring contract entry approval at or above the ADM level. The ADM will approve procurement plans for complexity levels 4 and 5. The formal procurement plan must be comprehensive and include a detailed risk assessment. The formal procurement plan must be prepared as a memorandum from either:

structure_blocks.text: The formal procurement plan follows a structured approach that promotes transparency, strategic alignment, and compliance with policies and guidelines. You must include the elements identified below to ensure you are well prepared before procurement initiation.

raw_text:
Procurement plan

A procurement plan outlines the intended strategy for conducting procurement activities prior to undertaking significant actions such as posting an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) or entering into contract negotiations.

The procurement plan is a key tool to be used in discussions and to review points for considering options like competitive versus sole source, and industrial and regional benefits.

Before issuing a solicitation of offers, you must obtain approval of the procurement plan at the appropriate level.

A Procurement Risk Assessment (PRA) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - PSPC only) must be performed prior to completing the procurement plan and be attached to it. The identification of risks supports the formulation of risk mitigation strategies.

There are two types of procurement plans:

Procurement plan for procurements at or below Director General (DG) approval level

Formal Procurement Plan Template for procurements at or above Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) level

Changes to the original procurement plan

If there are significant changes to the original procurement plan you must:

review the complexity level

perform a new PRA

submit the revised procurement plan for approval

Note : If required, you may consult the group responsible for providing Risk Management Advisory Services (RMAS) .

For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level

A procurement plan form is used for procurements estimated to be at or below DG approval when there is insufficient information to obtain advance approval for contract entry.

For procurements outside the Department’s authority, the procurement plan must be prepared as a memorandum from the Senior Director (or Director) to the Director General (DG) or Regional Director General (RDG). It must include the elements identified under the formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM level.

The procurement plan must provide details on medium-high to high risks and proposed mitigation strategies. You may also refer to Determine the procurement strategy for a list of items that could be included in the procurement plan.

There are two steps to this process:

Submission to the designated authority at or below the DG level for review of the procurement plan and the PRA , addressing comprehensive risk identification and mitigation

Formal approval by the DG or their authorized representative, ensuring that all procedural and strategic aspects of the procurement are adequately addressed and documented

Formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM approval level

The formal procurement plan is required for the approval of the procurement strategy for all Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Acquisitions Program (AP) procurements requiring contract entry approval at or above the ADM level. The ADM will approve procurement plans for complexity levels 4 and 5. The formal procurement plan must be comprehensive and include a detailed risk assessment. The formal procurement plan must be prepared as a memorandum from either:

the Senior Director (or Director) to the DG or Regional Director General (RDG)

the DG or RDG to the ADM

The formal procurement plan follows a structured approach that promotes transparency, strategic alignment, and compliance with policies and guidelines. You must include the elements identified below to ensure you are well prepared before procurement initiation.

Description

A brief description of the requirement, including any options, advance approvals, and set-aside amounts. Indicate the period of the proposed contract and options where applicable.

Background

Background information providing context and addressing any relevant recommendations or decisions specific to the requirement, for example, audit recommendations, Canadian International Trade Tribunal determinations or Treasury Board (TB) decisions.

Estimated cost and name of client

Estimated cost including goods and services tax (GST) or harmonized sales tax (HST). Include any options, set-aside amounts, etc., and state which client department it is chargeable to.

Anticipated contract (or SO or SA ) approval authority required

Anticipated contract approval authority and the rationale for its selection.

Sourcing

Consider the following when selecting suppliers:

the applicable trade agreements, Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements, and policies governing sourcing decisions (for example, Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business, Canadian Content Policy, shipbuilding repair, refit and modernization, Policy on Green Procurement, Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement )

the sourcing strategy, such as the tender opportunities page, other public advertising, source lists (one time, permanent)

the justification for the decision to use sole source, if applicable

for procurement plans for which TB submissions will be required, the plan for a competitive or non-competitive contract must provide an assessment of the extent to which TB approval of the proposals could limit, or encourage, the competitiveness of related procurement activities in the future

Policy issues

Outline any special or unusual aspects of the procurement, for example, a deviation from policy. Recommend course of action to resolve or handle any problems involving potential major risks or deviations from sourcing policy or other applicable policy.

Note: Where there are major risks inherent in the proposed approach, PSPC procurement plans must be examined in consultation with PSPC financial authorities and Legal Services

Evaluation and contractor selection methodology

The following methodology must be described:

evaluation and contractor selection methodology, including evaluation criteria and selection methods

milestones targeting important dates in the procurement process, should be described in general terms rather than specific dates

additional comments relevant to the approval authority's consideration

Anticipated basis of payment

Provide the anticipated basis of payment to be used in the contract (for example, firm price, fixed time rates, ceiling price, limitation of expenditure). Include an explanation for the use of advance payments, and indicate when financial security (for example, surety bond, irrevocable standby letter of credit) will be required from the contractor. Address contract audit provisions.

Milestones

Give target dates for key milestones (for example, solicitation of offers, contract award, delivery schedule) in the form of "early May" or "mid August" or "late October", rather than specific dates.

Additional comments

Include any information that should be brought to the approval authority's attention, for example, limitation of contractor's liability, fairness monitor, industrial security requirements, intellectual property, business line consultations, green procurement.

Buyer details

Provide your contact information, identifying a clear point of contact for the procurement.

Additional comments

Leave two or three lines for comments by the approval authority.

Recommendations and formal approvals

Each procurement plan concludes with a section for recommendations and formal approvals. This is to ensure that the plan is thoroughly reviewed by the appropriate departmental authorities.

The approval process for high-complexity procurements involves:

Preparation of a formal procurement plan that includes an in-depth risk assessment and proposed mitigation strategies for medium to high risks

Formal approval by the ADM or higher authority, following a thorough review process that ensures the procurement strategy aligns with departmental and government-wide procurement policies and objectives

Annex A: Sole source questions and answers

If applicable, add the sole source questions and answers as Annex A.
markdown_text:
Procurement plan

A procurement plan outlines the intended strategy for conducting procurement activities prior to undertaking significant actions such as posting an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) or entering into contract negotiations.

The procurement plan is a key tool to be used in discussions and to review points for considering options like competitive versus sole source, and industrial and regional benefits.

Before issuing a solicitation of offers, you must obtain approval of the procurement plan at the appropriate level.

A Procurement Risk Assessment (PRA) (accessible only on the Government of Canada network - PSPC only) must be performed prior to completing the procurement plan and be attached to it. The identification of risks supports the formulation of risk mitigation strategies.

There are two types of procurement plans:

Procurement plan for procurements at or below Director General (DG) approval level

Formal Procurement Plan Template for procurements at or above Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) level

Changes to the original procurement plan

If there are significant changes to the original procurement plan you must:

review the complexity level

perform a new PRA

submit the revised procurement plan for approval

Note : If required, you may consult the group responsible for providing Risk Management Advisory Services (RMAS) .

For PSPC procurements up to Director General approval level

A procurement plan form is used for procurements estimated to be at or below DG approval when there is insufficient information to obtain advance approval for contract entry.

For procurements outside the Department’s authority, the procurement plan must be prepared as a memorandum from the Senior Director (or Director) to the Director General (DG) or Regional Director General (RDG). It must include the elements identified under the formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM level.

The procurement plan must provide details on medium-high to high risks and proposed mitigation strategies. You may also refer to Determine the procurement strategy for a list of items that could be included in the procurement plan.

There are two steps to this process:

Submission to the designated authority at or below the DG level for review of the procurement plan and the PRA , addressing comprehensive risk identification and mitigation

Formal approval by the DG or their authorized representative, ensuring that all procedural and strategic aspects of the procurement are adequately addressed and documented

Formal procurement plan for procurements at or above the ADM approval level

The formal procurement plan is required for the approval of the procurement strategy for all Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) Acquisitions Program (AP) procurements requiring contract entry approval at or above the ADM level. The ADM will approve procurement plans for complexity levels 4 and 5. The formal procurement plan must be comprehensive and include a detailed risk assessment. The formal procurement plan must be prepared as a memorandum from either:

the Senior Director (or Director) to the DG or Regional Director General (RDG)

the DG or RDG to the ADM

The formal procurement plan follows a structured approach that promotes transparency, strategic alignment, and compliance with policies and guidelines. You must include the elements identified below to ensure you are well prepared before procurement initiation.

Description

A brief description of the requirement, including any options, advance approvals, and set-aside amounts. Indicate the period of the proposed contract and options where applicable.

Background

Background information providing context and addressing any relevant recommendations or decisions specific to the requirement, for example, audit recommendations, Canadian International Trade Tribunal determinations or Treasury Board (TB) decisions.

Estimated cost and name of client

Estimated cost including goods and services tax (GST) or harmonized sales tax (HST). Include any options, set-aside amounts, etc., and state which client department it is chargeable to.

Anticipated contract (or SO or SA ) approval authority required

Anticipated contract approval authority and the rationale for its selection.

Sourcing

Consider the following when selecting suppliers:

the applicable trade agreements, Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements, and policies governing sourcing decisions (for example, Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business, Canadian Content Policy, shipbuilding repair, refit and modernization, Policy on Green Procurement, Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement )

the sourcing strategy, such as the tender opportunities page, other public advertising, source lists (one time, permanent)

the justification for the decision to use sole source, if applicable

for procurement plans for which TB submissions will be required, the plan for a competitive or non-competitive contract must provide an assessment of the extent to which TB approval of the proposals could limit, or encourage, the competitiveness of related procurement activities in the future

Policy issues

Outline any special or unusual aspects of the procurement, for example, a deviation from policy. Recommend course of action to resolve or handle any problems involving potential major risks or deviations from sourcing policy or other applicable policy.

Note: Where there are major risks inherent in the proposed approach, PSPC procurement plans must be examined in consultation with PSPC financial authorities and Legal Services

Evaluation and contractor selection methodology

The following methodology must be described:

evaluation and contractor selection methodology, including evaluation criteria and selection methods

milestones targeting important dates in the procurement process, should be described in general terms rather than specific dates

additional comments relevant to the approval authority's consideration

Anticipated basis of payment

Provide the anticipated basis of payment to be used in the contract (for example, firm price, fixed time rates, ceiling price, limitation of expenditure). Include an explanation for the use of advance payments, and indicate when financial security (for example, surety bond, irrevocable standby letter of credit) will be required from the contractor. Address contract audit provisions.

Milestones

Give target dates for key milestones (for example, solicitation of offers, contract award, delivery schedule) in the form of "early May" or "mid August" or "late October", rather than specific dates.

Additional comments

Include any information that should be brought to the approval authority's attention, for example, limitation of contractor's liability, fairness monitor, industrial security requirements, intellectual property, business line consultations, green procurement.

Buyer details

Provide your contact information, identifying a clear point of contact for the procurement.

Additional comments

Leave two or three lines for comments by the approval authority.

Recommendations and formal approvals

Each procurement plan concludes with a section for recommendations and formal approvals. This is to ensure that the plan is thoroughly reviewed by the appropriate departmental authorities.

The approval process for high-complexity procurements involves:

Preparation of a formal procurement plan that includes an in-depth risk assessment and proposed mitigation strategies for medium to high risks

Formal approval by the ADM or higher authority, following a thorough review process that ensures the procurement strategy aligns with departmental and government-wide procurement policies and objectives

Annex A: Sole source questions and answers

If applicable, add the sole source questions and answers as Annex A.
